title: Wax-resist double-glaze overlap
subtitle: A base glaze, brushed wax-resist pattern, and a contrast glaze fired together at cone 6
excerpt: Wax-resist double-glazing builds a pattern by alternating two glazes through a wax-resist mask. The base glaze goes on first; wax is brushed in a pattern over the dried base glaze; the contrast glaze goes on over the wax. In the firing the wax burns off and the contrast glaze adheres only where the base was uncovered. The result is a two-glaze surface with sharp pattern edges. This tutorial covers a cone 6 stoneware piece with two commercial mid-fire glazes.
sourceNotes: Wax-resist glaze layering technique derived from first principles and standard studio practice. V&A open-access collection for the historical-form reference on wax-resist patterning in studio pottery. Orton Ceramic Foundation cone reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pottery fired once at cone 06 (999°C). Hard and porous; absorbs water from glaze on contact.
Water-based wax emulsion brushed onto bisque or onto a dried glaze layer to mask areas that a subsequent glaze should not adhere to. Burns out cleanly in the firing.
The second kiln firing at the body's mature temperature (cone 6 / 1222°C for stoneware) that melts the glaze and vitrifies the body.
The first glaze layer applied to the bisque piece. The wax resist pattern sits on top of this layer; the contrast glaze sits on top of the wax.
The second glaze layer, applied over the wax pattern. Where the wax covers the base glaze, the contrast glaze beads off and falls clear. The contrast glaze remains only on the unwaxed areas.

projectSchedule.title: Wax the foot ring and apply base glaze
projectSchedule.body: Wax the foot ring of the bisque piece. Dip in the base glaze. Allow to dry to the touch; usually an hour.

projectSchedule.title: Brush the wax pattern over the dried base glaze
projectSchedule.body: Once the base glaze is dry, brush the wax resist in the chosen pattern (stripes, dots, organic shapes) over the surface. Wax must fully cover the base glaze where pattern is wanted.

projectSchedule.title: Allow the wax to dry overnight
projectSchedule.body: Wax dries to the touch in 20-30 minutes but cures fully overnight. A fully cured wax repels the contrast glaze cleanly.

projectSchedule.title: Apply the contrast glaze
projectSchedule.body: Dip or brush the contrast glaze over the waxed surface. The wax resists; glaze beads off where the wax is. Wipe off any beaded contrast glaze before it dries.

projectSchedule.title: Glaze fire at cone 6
projectSchedule.body: Load on a kiln-washed shelf. Fire to cone 6 (1222°C). Wax burns off cleanly between 200-400°C, leaving base glaze in the masked areas and contrast glaze elsewhere.

content.text: Wax-resist double-glazing builds a two-glaze pattern in three layers: base glaze first, wax pattern on top of the dried base glaze, contrast glaze over everything. In the firing the wax burns off cleanly and the contrast glaze adheres only where the wax did not cover the base. The result has a hard pattern edge that no brushwork can match. The technique works on any bisque piece with a smooth surface; a mug, a bowl, a plate, or a vase all suit. Both kiln access (for the glaze firing) and a kiln-washed shelf are required.

attrs.title: Silica + kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powder is open. Wet-clean only. For the kiln firing: ensure ventilation by downdraft vent or by an open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Wax burns off between 200-400°C and produces visible smoke; the kiln area must be ventilated through this phase. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.

items.name: Contrast glaze (cone 6 mid-fire, pre-mixed)
items.substitution: any compatible cone 6 glaze with strong visual contrast

items.name: Needle tool
items.substitution: for clearing beaded contrast glaze off the wax

content.text: Choosing the two glazes

Both glazes must be fired at the same cone. Mixing a cone 6 base with a cone 10 contrast results in one or the other being underfired or overfired. Choose two pre-mixed glazes from the same supplier and at the same cone target. For visual contrast, pair a light glaze (white, pale celadon, soft yellow) with a darker glaze (deep blue, iron-rich brown, dark green). Two glazes of similar colour will fire to a similar finished surface and the pattern will be hard to read.

Some glazes do not layer well; a high-flux glaze on top of a low-flux glaze may run; a matte over a glossy may craze. Test the combination on a tile before committing to the finished piece. See the test-tile tutorial for the standard test protocol.
Preparing the bisque
Wipe the
bisqueware
with a damp sponge to remove kiln dust. Apply
wax resist
to the foot ring with a dedicated brush. Allow the wax to dry for 20-30 minutes before continuing.
Applying the base glaze
Stir the
base glaze

thoroughly. Dip the piece by the waxed foot ring for two seconds, rotate as it lifts clear, drain back into the bucket. Touch up the finger marks on the foot ring with a liner brush loaded with glaze. The piece now has an even base glaze coat across the body surface; the foot ring is clean.

Set the piece on a rack and allow the base glaze to dry to the touch; usually an hour in normal studio conditions. The surface should feel dry and chalky, not damp. Wax brushed onto damp glaze beads up and does not adhere cleanly.
Brushing the wax pattern

Sketch the pattern lightly on the dry base glaze with a pencil or by eye if confident. Common starting patterns: horizontal stripes around the body, vertical stripes from rim to base, scattered dots, organic shapes (leaves, branches, plain silhouettes). Avoid a first pattern with very fine detail; the wax brush wants 2-3 mm minimum line width to flow cleanly.

Load the liner brush with wax (or use the mop or hake brush for broad fills). Brush the wax onto the base glaze in a single confident stroke per line. The wax should leave a glossy line that fully covers the base glaze underneath. Patchy or thin wax allows the contrast glaze to grip in the gaps and the pattern edge becomes ragged in firing.

Clean the brush in cold water as soon as the wax application is done. Wax dries in the brush within an hour and is hard to remove once cured. A dedicated brush kept only for wax is the studio convention.

Allow the wax to cure overnight. Wax dries to the touch in 20-30 minutes but does not fully cure for several hours. A fully cured wax repels the contrast glaze; a partially cured wax allows the contrast glaze to seep in at the edges.
Applying the contrast glaze
Stir the
contrast glaze

thoroughly. Dip the piece in the contrast glaze bucket as before; same protocol, two-second submerge, rotate on lift, drain back. The glaze adheres to the unwaxed base glaze cleanly and beads up on the waxed pattern. The beaded contrast glaze sits in droplets on top of the wax pattern.

While the glaze is still wet, wipe the beaded droplets off the wax with a clean damp sponge or the needle tool. Most of the beaded glaze comes off easily; a small amount may remain in the surface texture of the wax. Take care not to scrub: light wiping is enough.

Set the piece on a rack and allow the contrast glaze to dry. The pattern is now visible: unwaxed areas show contrast-glaze chalky surface; waxed areas show base-glaze chalky surface with residual wax visible as a slight glossy finish.
Glaze firing

Load the piece on a freshly kiln-washed shelf with at least 50 mm of clearance to any neighbouring work; the contrast glaze beads that did not get wiped fully may run slightly in firing and a clearance avoids fusing pieces together.
Glaze fire

to cone 6 (1222°C) following the kiln manufacturer's cone 6 schedule. The wax burns off cleanly between 200-400°C as the kiln climbs through the first phase; expect visible smoke and a faint smell during this window.
Cool with the lid closed until the kiln reads 80°C or below before opening.
What it looks like when it is right

The finished piece shows a two-glaze surface with clean pattern edges. The base glaze shows through where the wax pattern was; the contrast glaze covers the rest of the surface. Both glazes are evenly fired with no bare patches and no over-applied runs. The pattern edges are sharp and slightly raised where the contrast glaze stops at the edge of the base layer underneath.
Variations

Three glazes can be stacked by waxing twice: base glaze, wax pattern A, second glaze, wax pattern B, third glaze. The technique compounds fast; two glazes is the working starting point for a first pattern. Latex resist can be substituted for wax where a thicker resist is needed or where the resist needs to be peeled away before firing rather than burned off; latex gives a different edge quality but requires removal before glazing.

items.symptom: Pattern edges are ragged or fuzzy in the fired piece
items.cause: Wax was applied too thin or had not fully cured before the contrast glaze went on
items.fix: Apply wax in a single thick confident stroke; allow wax to cure overnight before applying the contrast glaze

items.symptom: Contrast glaze adheres in patches where the wax pattern should have masked
items.cause: Wax did not fully cover the base glaze in those spots, or the wax was thinned with water
items.fix: Use wax resist undiluted; load the brush fully on each stroke; visually check coverage before drying

items.symptom: Two glazes fused into an indistinct mix at the boundary
items.cause: Beaded contrast glaze was not wiped off the wax pattern before drying
items.fix: Wipe beaded droplets immediately after the contrast glaze application while still wet; a clean damp sponge or needle tool clears the droplets

items.symptom: Glaze ran badly during firing and contrast pooled at the base
items.cause: Glazes incompatible at cone 6; one glaze too fluxed for the other
items.fix: Test glaze combinations on test tiles before committing to the finished piece; if persistent, switch to a less-fluxed contrast glaze

items.symptom: Pinholes through the pattern after firing
items.cause: Glaze trapped air or wax burned out incompletely
items.fix: Make sure the base glaze is fully dry before waxing. Slow the firing climb between 200-400°C. This gives the wax time to burn off so no carbon gets trapped under the second glaze
